romania.title: Colegiul Medicilor reacționeză la panourile cu mesaje false anti-cezariană: „Dezinformarea în sănătate nu reprezintă simple opinii”
romania.summary: Afirmațiile afișate pe zecile de panouri publicitare apărute recent în București, ce promovează mesaje false anti-cezariană, „sunt lipsite de fundament științific”, spun membrii Comisiei de Obstetrică-Ginecologie a Colegiului Medicilor. „Nașterea prin operație cezariană reprezintă, în numeroase situații, o intervenție medicală salvatoare de viață, atât pentru mamă, cât și pentru făt, fiind indicată în baza unor …

romania.title: Lungmetrajul „Jurnalul unei cameriste”, scris și regizat de Radu Jude, în selecția Quinzaine des Cinéastes de la Cannes 2026
romania.summary: Cel mai nou film scris și regizat de Radu Jude, „Jurnalul unei cameriste”, a fost inclus în selecția oficială a celei de-a 58-a ediții a secțiunii Quinzaine des Cinéastes din cadrul Festivalul de Film de la Cannes.

romania.title: Radu Jude va concura la Cannes cu noul său film, „Jurnalul unei cameriste”: „Nu este o adaptare a romanului omonim”
romania.summary: „Jurnalul unei cameriste”, cel mai nou film scris și regizat de Radu Jude, a fost inclus în selecția oficială a celei de-a 58-a ediții a secțiunii „Quinzaine des Cinéastes” de la Festivalul Internațional de Film de la Cannes. „Noul meu film, ‘Le Journal d’une femme de chambre’ (Jurnalul unei cameriste), nu este nici o adaptare …

romania.title: Viitorul este fabricat în România cu investiții în oameni și tehnologie. Philip Morris International, Angajator de Top pentru al 11-lea an consecutiv
romania.summary: „Obținerea certificării Top Employer reflectă angajamentul nostru de a crea un mediu în care oamenii pot crește, colabora și inova cu încredere.”, spune Gerasimos Contoguris, Director People & Culture, Philip Morris România. „Dezvoltarea personală și profesională se află în ADN-ul Philip Morris International, iar fiecare coleg este încurajat să-și asume parcursul profesional, să își exprime …

romania.summary: Încă nu e clar cine e în spatele afişelor împotriva naşterii prin cezariană, dar un lucru e clar: i-a furat imaginea lui Marcel! Bărbatul de 44 de ani din cartierul Berceni, posesor al unei impresionante burţi de bere, spune că pozele lui de pe Facebook au fost folosite fără permisiune în cadrul campaniei de propagandă. romania.summary: În mediul digital contemporan, problema nu mai este ce informație este adevărată și ce informație este falsă. Problema este că nu mai vedem aceeași realitate. În perioade electorale, această diferență… Articolul CERCETARE: Democrația filtrată, studiul românesc care arată că nu votăm pe baza realității, ci pe baza unei realități construite de algoritmi apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

La data de 14 aprilie 2026, cu 53 de zile înainte de evenimentul programat în 6… Articolul Swimathon Oradea 2026 prinde contur: proiectele comunității au intrat în linie dreaptă, iar implicarea publicului poate face diferența apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Târgul are loc la Pavilionul ISHO (Bulevardul Take Ionescu… Articolul Harababura Fair: Design românesc și Record Store Day, la Timișoara apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: În perioada Postului Mare, peste 100 de familii din județul Prahova au primit pachete cu alimente de bază din partea voluntarilor Paraclisului Catedralei Naționale. Cea mai recentă acțiune a campaniei… Articolul Peste 100 de familii din județul Prahova au primit alimente de bază apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Editura ap! (ACT și Politon) lansează Expunerea: Viața lui Iceman în fotografii Înainte de recorduri sau studii științifice, există o poveste marcată de eforturi supraomenești, prietenie, pierdere, antrenament, angajamente zilnice… Articolul Recorduri mondiale, disciplină și pierdere: Expunerea, o nouă carte despre curaj, frig și depășirea limitelor umane, de Wim Hof apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu: „Măcar să piardă unul de-al lor!”
romania.summary: În timp ce Budapesta sărbătoreşte, la Bucureşti e doliu: românul nostru a pierdut fără drept de apel alegerile din Ungaria. Sună incredibil, dar e adevărat: în urmă cu câteva zile, presimţindu-şi înfrângerea, Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu. „Am zis: măcar să piardă unul de-al lor”, şi-a motivat fostul premier maghiar decizia, pe […] The post Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu: „Măcar să piardă unul de-al lor!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

romania.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
romania.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
romania.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

romania.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

romania.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
romania.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

medical.title: Combining new drug with chemotherapy extends survival of platinum-resistant ovarian cancer patients in clinical trial
medical.summary: Platinum-based chemotherapy is a standard treatment for ovarian cancer, but its effectiveness can be limited in some cases. In some patients, the disease returns or progresses within six months of the last platinum dose, a condition referred to as platinum-resistant cancer. This form of cancer is a leading cause of gynecological cancer-related mortality.

medical.title: Blast exposure linked to brain aneurysms in U.S. Special Operations Forces
medical.summary: In a large sample of United States (U.S.) Special Operations Forces personnel, researchers found that a higher prevalence of intracranial aneurysms was independently associated with greater repeated blast exposure. Results of the study were published in Radiology.

medical.title: People who consume ultra-processed foods have worse muscle health, study suggests
medical.summary: Researchers found that a diet high in ultra-processed foods is associated with higher amounts of fat stored inside thigh muscles, regardless of calorie or fat intake, physical activity or sociodemographic factors in a population at risk for knee osteoarthritis. Results of the study were published in Radiology. Higher amounts of intramuscular fat in the thigh could potentially increase the risk for knee osteoarthritis.

medical.title: Researchers discover what may be the root cause of preeclampsia—and how to fix it
medical.summary: Preeclampsia is a life-threatening pregnancy complication that affects roughly 1 in 10 pregnancies worldwide and, in the United States alone, around 5%–8% of pregnancies. The condition can lead to severe, long-term health consequences for mothers and their babies. Despite its prevalence, though, the underlying cause has remained largely unknown.

medical.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
medical.summary: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

medical.title: Why fasting can lead to a longer lifespan
medical.summary: Restricting calories has long been recognized as a powerful way to live longer, with periods of intermittent fasting proving more effective than a steady diet. However, the mechanism behind this phenomenon has been unclear. Research led by UT Southwestern Medical Center scientists and published in Nature Communications suggests it's not the fast itself that extends life, but how the body metabolically pivots during refeeding after fasting. Although the findings were made in Caenorhabditis elegans, a roundworm often used as a lab model, they could eventually lead to new ways to boost health in humans.

medical.title: What does the new £1bn investment in community energy really mean?
medical.summary: It’s the largest public investment in community-owned green power in the UK to date. But how can communities get involved? The post What does the new £1bn investment in community energy really mean? appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Blocking a single protein supercharges the immune system against cancer
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to supercharge the immune system’s T cells by blocking a protein called Ant2, forcing the cells to rewire how they generate energy. This shift makes them more powerful, resilient, and effective at finding and destroying cancer cells.

medical.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don’t decline faster, study finds
medical.summary: Loneliness may quietly affect how well older adults remember things—but it might not be speeding up mental decline after all. A large European study tracking over 10,000 people for seven years found that those who felt lonelier started off with weaker memory, yet their memory didn’t deteriorate any faster than those who felt more socially connected. The findings challenge the idea that loneliness directly accelerates cognitive decline or dementia, suggesting instead that it impacts baseline brain performance.

medical.title: This strange “pearling” motion inside cells could change how we understand disease
medical.summary: Mitochondria don’t just generate energy—they also carefully organize their own DNA in a surprisingly elegant way. Scientists have discovered that a long-overlooked phenomenon called “mitochondrial pearling,” where mitochondria briefly form bead-like shapes, helps evenly space clusters of mitochondrial DNA.

medical.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
medical.summary: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange. The same process may be happening in human households through everyday closeness.

medical.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
medical.summary: Astrocytes, once thought to be mere brain “support cells,” are now revealed to be key players in fear memory. Researchers found they actively help form, recall, and weaken fear responses by interacting with neurons in real time. Changing astrocyte activity directly altered how strong fear memories became. This breakthrough could lead to entirely new treatments for anxiety-related disorders.

medical.title: What went right this week: a ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, plus more
medical.summary: A ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, Africa’s road safety ‘milestone’ and a quantum leap for solar, plus more The post What went right this week: a ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: Droplet impacts reveal surprising physics in shear-thickening fluids
science.summary: From ketchup to quicksand, non-Newtonian fluids have long fascinated and puzzled scientists. Unlike ordinary fluids, their flow properties change depending on how much force is applied, but the precise mechanics governing this behavior remain poorly understood—particularly under rapid deformation. Now, a team led by Xiang Cheng at the University of Minnesota has used droplet impacts to probe these dynamics in new detail, uncovering behaviors which have eluded physicists so far. Their findings appear in Physical Review Letters.

science.title: The quietest place we've ever listened from
science.summary: We have been searching for signals from other civilizations for over sixty years. Radio telescopes on Earth have swept the sky, listened patiently, and found nothing but silence. It is a search that demands extraordinary sensitivity and that is the problem. Earth and our very existence itself are getting in the way.

science.title: Mammal ancestors laid eggs, and this 250-million-year-old fossil finally proves it
science.summary: In the aftermath of Earth’s most catastrophic extinction event, one unlikely survivor rose to dominate a shattered world: Lystrosaurus. Now, a stunning fossil discovery—an ancient egg containing a curled-up embryo—has finally answered a decades-old mystery about whether mammal ancestors laid eggs. Using advanced imaging technology, scientists confirmed that these resilient creatures did reproduce this way, likely producing large, soft-shelled eggs packed with nutrients.

science.title: AI spots hidden behavior patterns in self-organizing bacteria
science.summary: Life moves in mysterious ways—and perhaps especially so for organisms that undergo dramatic shifts in levels of self-organization, such as Myxococcus xanthus. A custom-built artificial intelligence system developed by Rice University researchers helped uncover how bacterial communities organize themselves, showing that the earliest moments of a biological transition carry far more information than previously considered.

science.title: Researchers unveil new AI-driven system set to transform coral reef restoration
science.summary: UK researchers have developed a first-of-its-kind bespoke AI system designed to assess coral health and detect early stress, helping to prevent restoration projects from failing. Led by PhD research student at the University of Derby, Nicole Yeomans, and marine scientist Professor Michael Sweet at the University of Derby, the system, named BlueBiome, is reimagining coral reef care by applying the same principles of preventive, precision health commonly used in human gut health.

science.title: Next-generation atomic clock successfully tested at sea
science.summary: Adelaide University researchers have successfully tested a new type of portable atomic clock at sea for the first time, using technology that could help power the next generation of navigation, communications and scientific systems. The research team, from the Institute for Photonics and Advanced Sensing (IPAS), developed the highly precise device and trialed it aboard a vessel provided by the Royal Australian Navy in July 2024. They have reported their findings in a new paper published in the journal Optica.

science.title: Nutrition Research Arrives Aboard Space Station
science.summary: No matter how far humanity aims to travel or how ambitious the mission, nutrition will play a key role for the crew members on distant worlds. Before planning long-term stays on the Moon, Mars, and beyond, humans must learn to grow and care for plants and other sources of nutrition like algae to keep the […]

science.title: NASA’s Webb Redefines Dividing Line Between Planets, Stars
science.summary: Planets, like those in our solar system, form in a bottom-up process where small bits of rock and ice clump together and grow larger over time. But the heftier the planet, the harder it is to explain its formation that way. Astronomers used NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope to examine 29 Cygni b, an object […]

science.title: Cosmic dust identified as the source of Venus' enigmatic lower haze
science.summary: Venus, often called Earth's twin, is in fact a planet of extremes. Beneath its thick carbon dioxide atmosphere are crushing surface temperatures and dense clouds of sulfuric acid. While the planet's main cloud layer sits between 47 and 70 kilometers above the surface, scientists have long been puzzled by a mysterious layer of particles below 47 kilometers, known as the "lower haze." First detected by spacecraft in the 1970s, the origin of this haze remained unexplained for more than half a century.

science.title: Four weeks of 'safe' low-level PFAS exposure in tap water altered embryo development in mice
science.summary: An Adelaide University study has revealed that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) found in tap water, at levels currently considered safe, affected the development of embryos, causing irreversible damage that may be passed on to future generations. The researchers examined the effects of tap water on female mice following daily consumption for four weeks or six months. The results, published in Environmental Research, showed exposure to PFAS chemicals, even in trace amounts, disrupts female fertility, causing cellular damage to ovulated eggs and embryos.

science.title: Bats on a break: Tracking the secret life of pond bats
science.summary: What do bats do at night when they're not hunting? Using tiny GPS trackers, Leiden researchers discovered that pond bats spend a substantial portion of the night resting—often outdoors. This surprising insight could change the way we protect them. "To rest or to roam: Functional habitat use of an insectivorous bat species during active and resting behavior" is published in Biological Conservation.

science.title: A 'blob' in a tank is helping scientists tease out the secrets of turbulence
science.summary: In a tank on the bottom floor of a University of Chicago research laboratory, scientists summon "The Blob" into existence by firing water jets to create an artfully choreographed series of rings.

science.title: Quantum systems can remember and forget at the same time, scientists discover
science.summary: Quantum systems can secretly “remember” their past—even when they appear not to. Scientists found that whether a system shows memory depends on how you look at it: through its evolving state or its measurable properties. Each perspective uncovers different kinds of memory, meaning a system can seem memoryless and memory-filled at the same time. This discovery could change how researchers design and control quantum technologies.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Cel mai nou film scris și regizat de Radu Jude, „Jurnalul unei cameriste”, a fost inclus în selecția oficială a celei de-a 58-a ediții a secțiunii Quinzaine des Cinéastes din cadrul Festivalul de Film de la Cannes.

mix_items.title: CERCETARE: Democrația filtrată, studiul românesc care arată că nu votăm pe baza realității, ci pe baza unei realități construite de algoritmi
mix_items.summary: În mediul digital contemporan, problema nu mai este ce informație este adevărată și ce informație este falsă. Problema este că nu mai vedem aceeași realitate. În perioade electorale, această diferență… Articolul CERCETARE: Democrația filtrată, studiul românesc care arată că nu votăm pe baza realității, ci pe baza unei realități construite de algoritmi apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Colegiul Medicilor reacționeză la panourile cu mesaje false anti-cezariană: „Dezinformarea în sănătate nu reprezintă simple opinii”
mix_items.summary: Afirmațiile afișate pe zecile de panouri publicitare apărute recent în București, ce promovează mesaje false anti-cezariană, „sunt lipsite de fundament științific”, spun membrii Comisiei de Obstetrică-Ginecologie a Colegiului Medicilor. „Nașterea prin operație cezariană reprezintă, în numeroase situații, o intervenție medicală salvatoare de viață, atât pentru mamă, cât și pentru făt, fiind indicată în baza unor …

La data de 14 aprilie 2026, cu 53 de zile înainte de evenimentul programat în 6… Articolul Swimathon Oradea 2026 prinde contur: proiectele comunității au intrat în linie dreaptă, iar implicarea publicului poate face diferența apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Harababura Fair: Design românesc și Record Store Day, la Timișoara
mix_items.summary: Timișoara găzduiește în perioada 17-19 aprilie, o nouă ediție Harababura Fair, eveniment dedicat designului românesc contemporan și culturii muzicii pe vinil. Târgul are loc la Pavilionul ISHO (Bulevardul Take Ionescu… Articolul Harababura Fair: Design românesc și Record Store Day, la Timișoara apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Peste 100 de familii din județul Prahova au primit alimente de bază
mix_items.summary: În perioada Postului Mare, peste 100 de familii din județul Prahova au primit pachete cu alimente de bază din partea voluntarilor Paraclisului Catedralei Naționale. Cea mai recentă acțiune a campaniei… Articolul Peste 100 de familii din județul Prahova au primit alimente de bază apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Recorduri mondiale, disciplină și pierdere: Expunerea, o nouă carte despre curaj, frig și depășirea limitelor umane, de Wim Hof
mix_items.summary: Editura ap! (ACT și Politon) lansează Expunerea: Viața lui Iceman în fotografii Înainte de recorduri sau studii științifice, există o poveste marcată de eforturi supraomenești, prietenie, pierdere, antrenament, angajamente zilnice… Articolul Recorduri mondiale, disciplină și pierdere: Expunerea, o nouă carte despre curaj, frig și depășirea limitelor umane, de Wim Hof apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu: „Măcar să piardă unul de-al lor!”
mix_items.summary: În timp ce Budapesta sărbătoreşte, la Bucureşti e doliu: românul nostru a pierdut fără drept de apel alegerile din Ungaria. Sună incredibil, dar e adevărat: în urmă cu câteva zile, presimţindu-şi înfrângerea, Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu. „Am zis: măcar să piardă unul de-al lor”, şi-a motivat fostul premier maghiar decizia, pe […] The post Viktor Orban şi-a schimbat numele în Vasile Românescu: „Măcar să piardă unul de-al lor!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Droplet impacts reveal surprising physics in shear-thickening fluids
mix_items.summary: From ketchup to quicksand, non-Newtonian fluids have long fascinated and puzzled scientists. Unlike ordinary fluids, their flow properties change depending on how much force is applied, but the precise mechanics governing this behavior remain poorly understood—particularly under rapid deformation. Now, a team led by Xiang Cheng at the University of Minnesota has used droplet impacts to probe these dynamics in new detail, uncovering behaviors which have eluded physicists so far. Their findings appear in Physical Review Letters.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: The quietest place we've ever listened from
mix_items.summary: We have been searching for signals from other civilizations for over sixty years. Radio telescopes on Earth have swept the sky, listened patiently, and found nothing but silence. It is a search that demands extraordinary sensitivity and that is the problem. Earth and our very existence itself are getting in the way.

mix_items.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
mix_items.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
mix_items.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

mix_items.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: Mammal ancestors laid eggs, and this 250-million-year-old fossil finally proves it
mix_items.summary: In the aftermath of Earth’s most catastrophic extinction event, one unlikely survivor rose to dominate a shattered world: Lystrosaurus. Now, a stunning fossil discovery—an ancient egg containing a curled-up embryo—has finally answered a decades-old mystery about whether mammal ancestors laid eggs. Using advanced imaging technology, scientists confirmed that these resilient creatures did reproduce this way, likely producing large, soft-shelled eggs packed with nutrients.

mix_items.title: Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil
mix_items.summary: Află povestea lui Ionuț și cum sprijinul educațional și emoțional oferit de Fundația Inocenți ajută copiii vulnerabili să își descopere încrederea în sine. The post Povestea lui Ionuț: cum sprijinul educațional schimbă viața unui copil appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: AI spots hidden behavior patterns in self-organizing bacteria
mix_items.summary: Life moves in mysterious ways—and perhaps especially so for organisms that undergo dramatic shifts in levels of self-organization, such as Myxococcus xanthus. A custom-built artificial intelligence system developed by Rice University researchers helped uncover how bacterial communities organize themselves, showing that the earliest moments of a biological transition carry far more information than previously considered.

mix_items.title: Combining new drug with chemotherapy extends survival of platinum-resistant ovarian cancer patients in clinical trial
mix_items.summary: Platinum-based chemotherapy is a standard treatment for ovarian cancer, but its effectiveness can be limited in some cases. In some patients, the disease returns or progresses within six months of the last platinum dose, a condition referred to as platinum-resistant cancer. This form of cancer is a leading cause of gynecological cancer-related mortality.

mix_items.title: Researchers unveil new AI-driven system set to transform coral reef restoration
mix_items.summary: UK researchers have developed a first-of-its-kind bespoke AI system designed to assess coral health and detect early stress, helping to prevent restoration projects from failing. Led by PhD research student at the University of Derby, Nicole Yeomans, and marine scientist Professor Michael Sweet at the University of Derby, the system, named BlueBiome, is reimagining coral reef care by applying the same principles of preventive, precision health commonly used in human gut health.

mix_items.title: Next-generation atomic clock successfully tested at sea
mix_items.summary: Adelaide University researchers have successfully tested a new type of portable atomic clock at sea for the first time, using technology that could help power the next generation of navigation, communications and scientific systems. The research team, from the Institute for Photonics and Advanced Sensing (IPAS), developed the highly precise device and trialed it aboard a vessel provided by the Royal Australian Navy in July 2024. They have reported their findings in a new paper published in the journal Optica.

mix_items.title: Blast exposure linked to brain aneurysms in U.S. Special Operations Forces
mix_items.summary: In a large sample of United States (U.S.) Special Operations Forces personnel, researchers found that a higher prevalence of intracranial aneurysms was independently associated with greater repeated blast exposure. Results of the study were published in Radiology.

mix_items.title: People who consume ultra-processed foods have worse muscle health, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that a diet high in ultra-processed foods is associated with higher amounts of fat stored inside thigh muscles, regardless of calorie or fat intake, physical activity or sociodemographic factors in a population at risk for knee osteoarthritis. Results of the study were published in Radiology. Higher amounts of intramuscular fat in the thigh could potentially increase the risk for knee osteoarthritis.

mix_items.title: Nutrition Research Arrives Aboard Space Station
mix_items.summary: No matter how far humanity aims to travel or how ambitious the mission, nutrition will play a key role for the crew members on distant worlds. Before planning long-term stays on the Moon, Mars, and beyond, humans must learn to grow and care for plants and other sources of nutrition like algae to keep the […]

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb Redefines Dividing Line Between Planets, Stars
mix_items.summary: Planets, like those in our solar system, form in a bottom-up process where small bits of rock and ice clump together and grow larger over time. But the heftier the planet, the harder it is to explain its formation that way. Astronomers used NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope to examine 29 Cygni b, an object […]

mix_items.title: Cosmic dust identified as the source of Venus' enigmatic lower haze
mix_items.summary: Venus, often called Earth's twin, is in fact a planet of extremes. Beneath its thick carbon dioxide atmosphere are crushing surface temperatures and dense clouds of sulfuric acid. While the planet's main cloud layer sits between 47 and 70 kilometers above the surface, scientists have long been puzzled by a mysterious layer of particles below 47 kilometers, known as the "lower haze." First detected by spacecraft in the 1970s, the origin of this haze remained unexplained for more than half a century.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover what may be the root cause of preeclampsia—and how to fix it
mix_items.summary: Preeclampsia is a life-threatening pregnancy complication that affects roughly 1 in 10 pregnancies worldwide and, in the United States alone, around 5%–8% of pregnancies. The condition can lead to severe, long-term health consequences for mothers and their babies. Despite its prevalence, though, the underlying cause has remained largely unknown.

mix_items.title: Four weeks of 'safe' low-level PFAS exposure in tap water altered embryo development in mice
mix_items.summary: An Adelaide University study has revealed that per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) found in tap water, at levels currently considered safe, affected the development of embryos, causing irreversible damage that may be passed on to future generations. The researchers examined the effects of tap water on female mice following daily consumption for four weeks or six months. The results, published in Environmental Research, showed exposure to PFAS chemicals, even in trace amounts, disrupts female fertility, causing cellular damage to ovulated eggs and embryos.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
mix_items.summary: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

mix_items.title: Bats on a break: Tracking the secret life of pond bats
mix_items.summary: What do bats do at night when they're not hunting? Using tiny GPS trackers, Leiden researchers discovered that pond bats spend a substantial portion of the night resting—often outdoors. This surprising insight could change the way we protect them. "To rest or to roam: Functional habitat use of an insectivorous bat species during active and resting behavior" is published in Biological Conservation.

mix_items.title: Why fasting can lead to a longer lifespan
mix_items.summary: Restricting calories has long been recognized as a powerful way to live longer, with periods of intermittent fasting proving more effective than a steady diet. However, the mechanism behind this phenomenon has been unclear. Research led by UT Southwestern Medical Center scientists and published in Nature Communications suggests it's not the fast itself that extends life, but how the body metabolically pivots during refeeding after fasting. Although the findings were made in Caenorhabditis elegans, a roundworm often used as a lab model, they could eventually lead to new ways to boost health in humans.

mix_items.title: What does the new £1bn investment in community energy really mean?
mix_items.summary: It’s the largest public investment in community-owned green power in the UK to date. But how can communities get involved? The post What does the new £1bn investment in community energy really mean? appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Blocking a single protein supercharges the immune system against cancer
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to supercharge the immune system’s T cells by blocking a protein called Ant2, forcing the cells to rewire how they generate energy. This shift makes them more powerful, resilient, and effective at finding and destroying cancer cells.

mix_items.title: A 'blob' in a tank is helping scientists tease out the secrets of turbulence
mix_items.summary: In a tank on the bottom floor of a University of Chicago research laboratory, scientists summon "The Blob" into existence by firing water jets to create an artfully choreographed series of rings.

mix_items.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don’t decline faster, study finds
mix_items.summary: Loneliness may quietly affect how well older adults remember things—but it might not be speeding up mental decline after all. A large European study tracking over 10,000 people for seven years found that those who felt lonelier started off with weaker memory, yet their memory didn’t deteriorate any faster than those who felt more socially connected. The findings challenge the idea that loneliness directly accelerates cognitive decline or dementia, suggesting instead that it impacts baseline brain performance.

mix_items.title: Quantum systems can remember and forget at the same time, scientists discover
mix_items.summary: Quantum systems can secretly “remember” their past—even when they appear not to. Scientists found that whether a system shows memory depends on how you look at it: through its evolving state or its measurable properties. Each perspective uncovers different kinds of memory, meaning a system can seem memoryless and memory-filled at the same time. This discovery could change how researchers design and control quantum technologies.

mix_items.title: This strange “pearling” motion inside cells could change how we understand disease
mix_items.summary: Mitochondria don’t just generate energy—they also carefully organize their own DNA in a surprisingly elegant way. Scientists have discovered that a long-overlooked phenomenon called “mitochondrial pearling,” where mitochondria briefly form bead-like shapes, helps evenly space clusters of mitochondrial DNA.

mix_items.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
mix_items.summary: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange. The same process may be happening in human households through everyday closeness.

mix_items.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
mix_items.summary: Astrocytes, once thought to be mere brain “support cells,” are now revealed to be key players in fear memory. Researchers found they actively help form, recall, and weaken fear responses by interacting with neurons in real time. Changing astrocyte activity directly altered how strong fear memories became. This breakthrough could lead to entirely new treatments for anxiety-related disorders.

mix_items.title: What went right this week: a ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, plus more
mix_items.summary: A ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, Africa’s road safety ‘milestone’ and a quantum leap for solar, plus more The post What went right this week: a ‘breakthrough’ for migratory species, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am verificat contul si el mi-a verificat caracterul.

satire_ro.title: Un succes! Clona AI a lui Mark Zuckerberg e de 3 ori mai umană decât originalul
